slides.title: The Backup Plan 🛟
slides.content:
# The `else` keyword

If the condition in our `if` statement is false, Ruby skips the code entirely.

But what if we want to run a designated "Plan B" when the condition fails? That's what `else` is for!

slides.title: Else in Action
slides.content: Run this! Since `money >= 20` is false, Ruby skips the `if` and runs the `else` code.
slides.codeSnippet:
money = 10

if money >= 20
  puts "I am buying a pizza!"
else
  puts "I guess I will eat pasta again..."
end

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: Think about the behavior of else.

quizData.question: When does the code inside an `else` block run?

options.text: Every time, regardless of what happens.
Only when the `if` condition evaluates to false.
Only when the `if` condition evaluates to true.

quizData.explanation: `else` acts as the default fallback. It only executes if every preceding condition was false.

slides.title: More Than Two Options 🔁
slides.content:
# The `elsif` keyword

Sometimes we have more than a simple Yes/No scenario. We might have three or four different possibilities.

You can chain conditions together using `elsif` (that's `else` and `if` mashed together)!

slides.title: Chaining Conditions
slides.content: Ruby will test these top-to-bottom. The first one that is `true` runs, and it ignores the rest! Change `light` to "yellow" to see what happens.
slides.codeSnippet:
light = "red"

if light == "green"
  puts "Walk!"
elsif light == "yellow"
  puts "Slow down!"
elsif light == "red"
  puts "Stop!"
else
  puts "The traffic light is broken!"
end

quizData.question: If multiple `elsif` conditions are 'true', how many of them will Ruby run?

options.text: All of them that are true.
Only the very first one that is true.

quizData.explanation: Ruby checks conditions from top to bottom. The moment it finds a `true` condition, it runs that block of code and entirely skips the rest of the chain.

slides.title: The Spelling Matters!
slides.content: A very common error for beginners transitioning from other languages: in Ruby, it is spelled `elsif` (with no 'e' in the middle), not `elseif`.
